t: — belongs to the category of postpartum psychosis (6E20 / 6A23.x).
30–50% of patients are re-diagnosed with schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, or bipolar disorder on long-term follow-up (Castagnini A. et al. Schizophr Res 2013).
Schizophrenia and bipolar disorder partially share genetic risk.
Risk increases when psychotic or affective disorder is present in family history.
Acute psychosocial stress (death, displacement, natural disaster) — identified in a significant proportion of patients; indicated as a stress qualifier in DSM-5-TR.
Postpartum period — hormonal and sleep disturbances.
Substance use — cannabis, amphetamine, hallucinogens — can trigger a psychotic episode, but substance-induced psychosis is coded as a separate category if the substance is specified.
Short-term sleep deprivation, intense emotional tension.
Temporary hyperactivity of the dopaminergic system — in the context of stress cortisol or hormonal changes.
Central mechanisms similar to schizophrenia, but shorter-term and potentially reversible.
Rapid onset — from premorbid normal function to full psychotic picture within days-weeks.
Polymorphic (variable) symptoms — delusions (persecution, relationship, religious), hallucinations (auditory, visual, cenesthetic), disorganized speech.
Emotional fluctuation — acute fear, ecstasy, panic, bewilderment.
Behavior — odd, disorganized; sometimes with catatonic components
Polymorphic psychotic disorder — without schizophrenia symptoms;
Polymorphic psychotic disorder — with schizophrenia symptoms;
Acute psychotic disorder like schizophrenia — Schneider first-rank symptoms dominant.

Typical course — peak achieved within 2–4 weeks, then gradual or rapid subsidence. Full recovery — within 1 month (DSM-5-TR) or 3 months (ICD-11). Persisting beyond this period leads to diagnosis transition to schizophreniform (DSM-5-TR 295.40, 1–6 months) or schizophrenia.
6.1 Unified diagnostic criteria (DSM-5-TR · ICD-11 consensus points)
A. Presence of one or more psychotic symptoms:
(At least one of 1, 2, or 3 must be present.)
, then full recovery to premorbid functional level.
ICD-11: full syndrome forms within 2 weeks;
— not fully explained by affective disorder with psychotic features, schizophrenia, schizoaffective disorder, substance-induced psychosis, organic psychosis.
Three subtypes – polymorphic (without/with schizophrenia symptoms) and schizophrenia-like. Postpartum onset is assigned to the separate code 6E20.
It is not a criterion, but a qualifier — compulsory in DSM-IV "Brief Reactive Psychosis.
Clinical interview + information from relatives — onset dynamics, premorbid function.
Toxicology screening (mandatory) — to rule out substance-induced psychosis.
Laboratory: complete blood count, liver, kidney, electrolytes, glucose, TSH, B12, syphilis, HIV.
EEG and brain MRI — first episode or atypical clinical presentation.
In postpartum context — thyroid function mandatory.
Assessment of suicide and child harm risk (in postpartum conditions).
Monitoring — may vary based on diagnosis duration (after 1 or 3 months elapsed).
Duration ≥ 1 month (ICD-11) / ≥ 6 months (DSM-5-TR general); residual symptoms.
An affective episode is present during a significant portion of the psychotic symptoms.
DSM-5-TR — 1–6 months; ICD-11 — no separate code.
In the context of a manic episode.
Within 4–6 weeks postpartum; high risk of infanticide and maternal suicide — medical emergency.
Clear temporal relationship with substance (cannabis, amphetamine, hallucinogen); toxicology screening.
Associated with organic cause (autoimmune encephalitis, lupus, paraneoplastic encephalitis).
Alteration in consciousness; attentional fluctuation; organic cause.
In trauma context; desocial isolation dominant.
HAM-D, YMRS — assessment of affective components.
According to first episode psychosis protocol (see 6A20 §7.2):
Absolute: complete blood count, liver/kidney, glucose, electrolytes, TSH, B12, folate, toxicology screening, HIV, syphilis, pregnancy test, prolactin.
If organic psychosis is suspected — anti-NMDA-R antibodies (CSF), ANA, lupus panel, paraneoplastic antibody panel, ammonia, ceruloplasmin.
In postpartum context — TSH (postpartum hypothyroidism possible).
Brain MRI — recommended in first episode (NICE CG178); especially with atypical presentation or neurological signs.
EEG — rule out seizures, atypical clinical presentation.
Lumbar puncture — suspected autoimmune encephalitis (atypical psychosis + motor symptoms + female age).
Urgent assessment and hospitalization if necessary
— for safety of patient or others.
First-line: atypical antipsychotic at low dose
— risperidone 1–4 mg, olanzapine 5–15 mg, quetiapine 200–600 mg, aripiprazole 5–15 mg.
— oral lorazepam 1–2 mg + oral disintegrating olanzapine 5–10 mg; intramuscular — olanzapine 10 mg im or aripiprazole 9.75 mg im, if necessary haloperidol 5 mg + lorazepam 2 mg im (classic).
Typical antipsychotic 3–6 months (after remission), then gradual reduction and monitoring; discontinuation based on first episode not standard — individual assessment.
— as a medical emergency; risk of child homicide and suicide is high; hospitalization, mother-child co-hospitalization conditions are preferred; see chapter 6E20.
— in refractory severe cases, with catatonic features, safest option during pregnancy.
30–50% of patients transition to long-term psychotic disorder; monthly in the first year, then quarterly.
first episode psychosis guidelines — low-dose atypical antipsychotic first line; early intervention services (EIP) involvement; psychosocial intervention combined.
There is no specific algorithm for acute and transient psychotic disorder; principles of first episode psychosis.
NICE CG192 (Antenatal and postnatal mental health):
Postpartum psychosis is a medical emergency — hospital admission, atypical antipsychotic + benzodiazepine, ECT in refractory cases.
